coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode cholesterol sensor based on gold nanorods and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Liyun
Wang, Shiyuan
Li, Qin
Zhang, Yumei
Zhao, Jue
Jiang, Xingdong
Ma, Fei
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-mode cholesterol biosensor was developed based on gold nanorods-carbon quantum dots-Cholesterol oxidase (AuNRs-CQDs-COD) complex synthesized by the cross-linking method. The specific reaction to/against cholesterol and cholesterol oxidase can produce hydrogen peroxide. Due to the etching effect between the hydrogen peroxide and AuNRs, the absorption peaks of AuNRs would drift so that the fluorescence quenching occurred because of the electronic charge transfer between the hydrogen peroxide and CQDs. The spectrum properties, structure and micromorphology of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Fluorescence and Ultraviolet–Visible absorption spectroscopy, respectively. There was a good linear correlation between the fluorescence response and absorption peak shift of AuNRs-CQDs-COD probe in cholesterol solution within the concentration in the range 0.1–1 mM, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.028 μM. This dual-mode cholesterol biosensor based on AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex has good anti-interference and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode cholesterol biosensing
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanorods
Colorimetry
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of cholesterol by the dual-mode probe and its mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of AuNRs (Inset image is the HR-TEM image of AuNRs). (b) Particle size statistics of AuNRs.
(a) The TEM image of AuNRs-CQDs complex. (b) UV–Vis–NIR spectra of AuNRs and CQDs-AuNRs. (c) Infrared spectra of CQDs, AuNRs and AuNRs-CQDs.
(a) UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of AuNRs-CQDs and AuNRs-CQDs-COD. (b) Infrared spectrograms of COD, AuNRs-CQDs and AuNRs-CQDs-COD. (c) Preparation of AuNRs-CQDs-COD composite.
The schematic diagram of AuNRs-CQDs-COD detecting cholesterol.

(a) Absorption spectrum changes and (b) absorption peak changes of cholesterol solution in the concentration range of 0.1–1 mm. (c) Fluorescence spectrum changes and (d) fluorescence intensity ratio changes of cholesterol solution in the concentration range of 0.1–1 mM.

Time resolved fluorescence spectra of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex before (blue) and after (red) cholesterol addition. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The influence of different interfering substances on (a) the absorption peak and (b) the fluorescence intensity of the sensing probe.
Determination of cholesterol in serum samples using the proposed sensor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Recovery (%) is the ratio of cholesterol concentration found in serum samples to the concentration of added cholesterol.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode cholesterol sensor based on gold nanorods and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Fiber Optic Sensing Technology and Networks

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center of Fiber Optic Sensing Technology and Networks, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-mode cholesterol biosensor was developed based on gold nanorods-carbon quantum dots-Cholesterol oxidase (AuNRs-CQDs-COD) complex synthesized by the cross-linking method. The specific reaction to/against cholesterol and cholesterol oxidase can produce hydrogen peroxide. Due to the etching effect between the hydrogen peroxide and AuNRs, the absorption peaks of AuNRs would drift so that the fluorescence quenching occurred because of the electronic charge transfer between the hydrogen peroxide and CQDs. The spectrum properties, structure and micromorphology of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Fluorescence and Ultraviolet–Visible absorption spectroscopy, respectively. There was a good linear correlation between the fluorescence response and absorption peak shift of AuNRs-CQDs-COD probe in cholesterol solution within the concentration in the range 0.1–1 mM, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.028 μM. This dual-mode cholesterol biosensor based on AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex has good anti-interference and selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol plays a crucial role in building cell membrane, maintaining normal cell activity and building cell structure of the human life cycle. Cholesterol is mainly found in the diet and is widely present in human and animal bodies, such as in liver, spleen, kidney and skin, especially brain tissue [1]. The normal cholesterol concentration in human body is generally 2.7–5.4 mM [2], while its concentration needs to be maintained in a stable range for healthy human body. There was reported that it will lead to anemia and weakened human resistance when the concentration of cholesterol is too low, which may cause the diseases such as hyperthyroidism, anemia, depression and decreased immunity [3]. When the concentration of cholesterol is too high, it will not only cause damage to blood vessels, but also increase the risk of coronary heart disease [4]. Therefore, the cholesterol detection, as one of the most important biomarkers of cardiovascular disease and hypertension, is crucial for clinical diagnosis and health management.

At present, there are many methods for the detection of cholesterol, including chromatography [5], electrochemical method [6], enzyme colorimetric method [7] and spectrophotometric method [8]. However, these methods have some general shortcomings, such as long reaction time, complex detection process and low sensitivity. For example, the color reaction of enzyme colorimetry method for detecting cholesterol is easy to be interfered, while the phenomenon of the spectrophotometry method is very weak, and the reproducibility of the chromatography method is poor, etc. Biosensor is a new type of sensor that uses biological molecules (enzymes, DNA, antibodies, etc.) as analytes to transform biological signals into detectable signals [9], which can overcome the shortcomings of the above detection methods.

Biosensors based on nanomaterials (such as gold nanoparticles, graphene oxide, etc.) integrate multiple disciplines, which can significantly improve the sensitivity and specificity of biological detection [10–12]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)have quantum size effect and unique fluorescence properties due to their unique surface modifiable properties and electronic structure [13] and become a research hotspot in the field of nanomaterials and biosensing. Gold nanorods (AuNRs), as anisotropic non-spherical nanoparticles, are easily affected by their size and surrounding media. Usually, AuNRs can be immobilized on the surface of biomolecules as markers to achieve the detection of biomolecules. It can also be chemically modified to control its binding, stability and selectivity to biomolecules [14–17]. In the aspect of biological sensing, the detection of single signal is often highly intrusive and susceptible to the influence of environmental factors or interfering substances in the detection environment [18]. The construction of dual-mode sensing combined with two different detection methods can reduce the influence of various interfering factors and improve the reliability and accuracy of detection results [19]. Therefore, it is of great significance to combine the fluorescent probes with AuNRs to construct the dual-mode biosensors.

CQDs can be bind to AuNRs through the chemical bonds without affecting the fluorescence properties of CQDs due to their surface modifiability. In this work, AuNRs-CQDs complex was prepared by combining CQDs and AuNRs through Au–S bond [20]. A dual-mode probe AuNRs-CQDs-COD was prepared to realize the dual signal detection of cholesterol based on the colorimetry and fluorescence methods, in which cholesterol oxidase (COD) and AuNRs-CQDs complex were fixed by the cross-linking method. This dual-mode detection method has the advantages of simplicity, rapidity, strong anti-interference and high selectivity in complex sample environment, and has good application prospects.

Citric acid, l-cysteine, hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, sodium oleate, silver nitrate, cholesterol, cholesterol oxidase (COD) and l-Ascorbic acid (AA) was purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) (AR), gold chloride acid, hydrochloric acid, ascorbic acid, sodium borohydride, glucose, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, uric acid, glycine, Triton X-100, isopropyl alcohol, deionized water, Horse Serum was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd.

The surface morphology and particle size of CQDs and AuNRs were characterized by a JEM-2100 F Transmission electron microscope (TEM). UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was collected by a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were tested with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500 Hitachi, Japan), and the infrared spectrograms were analyzed by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, USA).

1.5 g citric acid and 0.57 g cysteine were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and reacted for 4 min with 700 W microwave to obtain a brown block substance. After dissolving it with a certain amount of deionized water, the solution was centrifuged for 8 min at speed of 5000 r/min to obtain supernatant. The supernatant was filtered several times with a 0.22 μm water filter membrane to remove the unreactants, and the solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight of 3500). After dialyzed for 24 h, the pure carbon quantum dots solution was obtained.

AuNRs were synthesized by the seed growth method. 20 μL of 25 mM chloroauric acid solution and 1 mL of 0.2 M Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) were mixed thoroughly, then 0.12 mL of 0.01 M NaBH4 was added and kept stirring for 2 min to get the required seed solution. 0.7 g of CTAB and 0.1234 g of sodium oleate were dissolved in 25 mL deionized water at 50 °C, and then cooled to 30 °C after being completely dissolved. 1.8 mL of 4 mM AgNO3 solution was added with stirring for 15 min, and then 1 mL of 25 mM chloroauric acid solution was added with stirring for 90 min, finally 150 μL of concentrated hydrochloric acid solution was added. After continuous stirring for 15 min, 125 μL of 0.064 mM AA solution was added, and 80 μL of seed solution was added after 30 s intense stirring. After stirring 30 s, AuNRs solution was kept at 30 °C for 12 h in the dark to obtain raw AuNRs solution. The raw AuNRs solution was centrifuged 6 times at speed of 10000 r/min to obtain the purified AuNRs solution.

AuNRs-CQDs complex was obtained by the reaction of AuNRs solution and CQDs solution at 30 °C for 15 min. EDC was selected as the cross-linking agent, and 2 mL AuNRs-CQDs complex was prepared by adding a certain amount of EDC cross-linking agent solution (dissolved in PBS buffer with pH = 7). After stirring for 1 h, quantitative COD was added and stirred for 5 h at low temperature to obtain AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex.

Five cholesterol solutions with the different concentrations in the range of 0.1–1 mM were prepared beforehand. 1.5 mL AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex solution was taken as the substrate, and the same amount of cholesterol solution with the different concentrations was added. After the substrate reacted with cholesterol completely, the corresponding absorption spectrum was obtained by UV–Vis spectrometer and the changes of absorbance and absorption peak were observed. The corresponding fluorescence spectrum was obtained by fluorescence spectrometer and its fluorescence intensity was recorded.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the TEM image of AuNRs. It can be seen that the dispersion of AuNRs is relatively uniform and the particle size is uniform. The length and width of a single AuNRs are about 70 nm and 20 nm, respectively. Fig. 1 (b) shows the layout of the aspect ratio of the AuNRs obtained by software statistics, and the average aspect ratio of the AuNRs is about 3.344.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the TEM image of AuNRs-CQDs complex. It can be seen that CQDs are clustered around the AuNRs, and closely arranged with a large of quantities. The optical characteristics and TEM image of CQDs have reported in reference [21]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of AuNRs and CQDs-AuNRs were tested as shown in Fig. 2 (b). The absorption peak of AuNRs was at 790 nm, and the absorption peak of AuNRs-CQDs complex was at 810 nm after AuNRs reacted with CQDs. The absorption peak was red shift about 20 nm due to the chemical reaction between Au and sulfhydryl group from the surface of CQDs, which will generate Au–S bond to form AuNRs-CQDs complex [22]. The reason is that the raw materials of preparing CQDs are citric acid and cysteine, where cysteine contains sulfhydryl group (-SH). The infrared spectra of AuNRs-CQDs complex, CQDs and AuNRs were characterized by FT-IR, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 (c). The infrared spectra of AuNRs-CQDs complex not only retained the absorption peak of CQDs in the range of 2800–2900 cm−1 (the absorption peak of 2850 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the C–H bond in alkanes), but also produced a new absorption peak in the range of 1400–1470 cm−1 (the absorption peak of 1463 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the C–H bending vibration peak of the carboxyl group (COOH)), and the absorption peak was significantly enhanced. It has confirmed the formation of AuNRs-CQDs complex and consistent with the results in Fig. 2 (a) and Fig. 2 (b).

UV–Vis–NIR and FT-IR spectra of AuNRs-CQDs and AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex were characterized as shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), respectively. It can be seen that the absorption peak of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex shows a significant red shift compared to AuNRs-CQDs complex after fixing COD. As COD is a macromolecular substance, the formation of Au–S bonds connected CQDs to AuNRs. After the reaction between COD and CQDs, the amide bond will be wrapped around CQDs, which affected the arrangement of AuNRs and changed the absorption peaks of AuNRs. As can be seen from Fig. 3 (b), the infrared spectra of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex and AuNRs-CQDs complex also are quite different, and AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex has obvious new peaks at 1660 cm−1 and 1542 cm−1, which is due to the bending vibration of amide I and amide II groups [23]. This indicates that COD successfully cross-linked with AuNRs-CQDs composite particles through amide bond. The possible reaction principal diagrams are shown in Fig. 3 (c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol by the dual-mode probe and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex was used to construct a dual-mode cholesterol biosensing probe based on the colorimetric method and the fluorescence method and the possible reaction principle diagrams are shown in Fig. 4 . Cholesterol is oxidized and decomposed into Cholest-4-en-3-one and H2O2 in the presence of COD and oxygen [24]. The generated products will affect the stability and dispersion of the complex system, leading to the aggregation of particles in the system so that changes in their absorption spectra. Moreover, H2O2 has the etching effect on AuNRs, further leading to the drift of the absorption peak of the complex system [25]. Under the excitation of 350 nm, the AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex was excited to emit the fluorescence. The enzyme catalyzed reaction between the substrate and the enzyme generates H2O2, which undergoes electron charge transfer with the AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex, thereby quenching the fluorescence of the AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex.

Absorption spectra of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex and corresponding absorption peak drifts at the difference of concentrations of cholesterol are shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). It can be seen in Fig. 5 (a), the absorption peak of the complex system was red shifted with the increase of the concentration of cholesterol solution. Fig. 5 (b) was obtained by taking cholesterol concentration as abscissa and absorption peak position as ordinate. The sensitivity of the cholesterol sensing probe was measured by the absorption peak and the variation of the absorption peak position with cholesterol solution concentration. When the concentration of cholesterol solution was in the range of 0.1–1 mM, the standard curve of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex for cholesterol detection was as follows:
(1)
λ
s p r
= 8 . 8
C h o l e s t e r o l
+ 808 . 22 ,
R 2
= 0 . 99
where, [cholesterol] was the concentration of cholesterol solution, λ
spr was the position of the absorption peak of the sample.

Fig. 5 (c) and Fig. 5 (d) show the fluorescence spectra of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex at the different concentrations of the cholesterol solution. The oxidation and decomposition of cholesterol can produce H2O2. Based on the electron charge transfer between H2O2 and CQDs [26], cholesterol has an obvious quenching effect on AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex. The fluorescence intensity of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex decreased with the increase of cholesterol concentration as shown in Fig. 5 (c) [27–29]. Therefore, AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex can be used as a cholesterol sensing probe to detect cholesterol concentration. The standard curve of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex for the detection of cholesterol solution in the concentration range of 0.1–1 mM as shown in Fig. 5 (d). The standard curve was as follows:
(2)
I 0
/ I = 0 . 1
C h o l e s t e r o l
+ 1 . 02 ,
R 2
= 0 . 92
where, I
0
/I was the ratio of fluorescence intensity of blank group without cholesterol and experimental group after cholesterol addition, and [cholesterol] was the concentration of cholesterol solution.

The limit of detection was determined using the formula D = 3
S / m , where S is the standard deviation of blank without the addition of cholesterol and m is the slope of the calibration curve. The performance of the proposed sensor was compared with other cholesterol biosensor as shown in Table 1 .

In order to verify the fluorescence quenching mechanism of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex dual-mode probe for cholesterol detection, the fluorescence lifetime of AuNRs-CQDs-COD dual-mode sensing probe before and after Enzyme catalysis was measured using time related single photon counting (TCSPC). The fluorescence lifetime curve is shown in Fig. 6 . Before and after the reaction between cholesterol and AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex, the two curves basically overlap. According to fitting calculations, the fluorescence lifetime of AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex without reaction is 9.67 ns. After adding cholesterol solution and fully reacting, the fluorescence lifetime is still 9.67 ns. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of cholesterol on AuNRs-CQDs-COD dual-mode sensing probe is static quenching. It means that the reaction of ground state fluorescent substance with quencher generates non-fluorescent compound, resulting in the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching [30,31].

Considering the detection of cholesterol and the environment in human body, the effects of some common biomolecules and ions in human body (ascorbic acid, Ca2+, K+, Na+, uric acid, citric acid, glucose, glycine) on the detection of AuNRs-CQDs-COD dual-mode sensing probe were studied. Fig. 7 (a) and (b) show the shift of absorption wavelength and the change of fluorescence intensity when the AuNRs-CQDs-COD dual-mode sensing probe detects these interfering substances, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), with the drift amount of cholesterol as the reference, the absorption peak of the AuNRs-CQDs-COD dual-mode sensing probe did not drift significantly under the same detection condition as cholesterol, and most of the interfering substances had no effect on AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex. Therefore, the cholesterol sensing probe has strong anti-interference in ultraviolet absorption detection. As shown in Fig. 7 (b), the ordinate is the fluorescence intensity of the blank group without the detection substance and the experimental group after the addition of the detection substance.

Taking the difference of fluorescence quenching of the cholesterol sensing probe by cholesterol as reference 1, the degree of fluorescence quenching of the probe by interfering substances is less than 0.2. Therefore, the interference of these common ions in human body to the cholesterol sensing probe is very small, which proves the good selectivity of the sensing system.

The vertical coordinates ( Δλ) of Fig. 7(a) is the result of standardization, which indicates the difference between the sensor signal (such as absorption peak shift) and the reference signal, without interference. Specifically, the formula for calculating Δλ is as follows:
(3)
Δ λ =
S
A b s
−
B
A b s
M
A b s
−
B
A b s
where S
Abs
is the absorption signal of the sensor, B
Abs
is the non-interference reference signal, and M
Abs
is the maximum value of the sensor signal. The reference signal here is usually measured under the condition of no interfering substances, and the maximum value is usually measured under the condition of adding a large number of interfering substances.

We have conducted a recovery experiment on serum samples. The initial cholesterol concentration in serum is set to 0 mg/mL. We use this sensor to detect the concentrations of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8 mg/mL cholesterol, and calculate the detection concentration of cholesterol by shifting the center wavelength of the sensor and obtaining the standard curve. All tests were conducted in triplicate measurements. The results are shown in Table 2 . The recovery rate of each concentration ranges from 95% to 105%, within the required range of clinical trials. The detection error of this sensor for serum samples is small, and the results are satisfactory.

A novel AuNRs-CQDs-COD dual-modal probe was synthesized successfully using the chemical cross-linking method. The probe AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex can detect cholesterol simultaneously based on the fluorescence and the colorimetric method. The principle of detection is the shift of absorption peaks caused by the etching effect of H2O2 on AuNRs, as well as the fluorescence quenching caused by the transfer of electronic charges between H2O2 and CQDs. The dual-mode sensor achieved the detection of cholesterol solution within the concentration in the range 0.1–1 mM with the LOD of 0.028 μM. The results indicate that the dual-mode sensing system has good anti-interference performance and can be applied to complex environments with strong interference.

Liyun Ding: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Shiyuan Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration. Qin Li: Visualization. Yumei Zhang: Methodology. Jue Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Xingdong Jiang: Resources, Supervision. Fei Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors disclosed no relevant relationships.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51878524), Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No.2021CFA041), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WUT:2021Ш028JC).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development an amperometric microbial-enzyme hybrid cholesterol biosensor based on ionic liquid MWCNT carbon paste electrode
Electroanalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Canbay, Erhan; Yasa, Ihsan; Akyilmaz, Erol. Development An Amperometric Microbial-Enzyme Hybrid Cholesterol Biosensor Based on Ionic Liquid MWCNT Carbon Paste Electrode[J]. Electroanalysis. 2021, 33, 11, 2381-2391.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Liyun Ding received her Ph.D. in materials science from Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, in 2007. She is currently a professor and doctoral supervisor at the School of Physical Science and Technology of Lanzhou University. Her research interest is in field of the optical fiber chemical and biosensors.

Shiyuan Wang received the B.Eng. degree from Hainan University, Hainan, in 2020. She is now a graduate student in Lanzhou University, Lanzhou. Her research interest is optical fiber chemical biosensor.

Qin Li received the B.Eng. degree from the Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, in 2019.She is now pursuing the master's degree with the Wuhan University of Technology. Her research interests include carbon quantum dots and optical fiber chemical biosensors.

Yumei Zhang received her B.Eng. degree from Henan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, in 2013 and graduated from Changchun University of Technology in 2019. She is now a graduate student in Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan. Her research interest is in optical fiber biosensors.

Fei Ma received his Ph. D in material physics and chemistry from Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, in 2008. He is now an associate professor in the School of Physical Science and Technology of Lanzhou University. His research interest is in field of the functional nanomaterials.

Jue Zhao received her Bachelor's degree in materials physics from Lanzhou University in 2002. She is now a lecturer at the School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University. Her research interest is in field of electromagnetic and optical experimental Measurements

Xingdong Jiang received his Ph.D. in particle physics and nuclear physics from Lanzhou University in 2019. He is now an engineer at the School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University. His research interests are in the ion irradiation effect of functional materials and the preparation and characterization of materials.
